items.subtopic: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Incidence and Mortality of Mesenteric Ischemia
items.content: Mesenteric ischemia affects 2–3 people per 100,000 annually, with a rising incidence in the aging population. The condition is divided into chronic mesenteric ischemia (CMI) and acute mesenteric ischemia (AMI). AMI has a high mortality rate between 50 and 80%, emphasizing the need for early diagnosis and prompt intervention.
keyPoints: Mesenteric ischemia affects 2–3 people per 100,000 annually.
Mortality with acute presentation remains high (between 50 and 80%).
Early diagnosis and prompt intervention are crucial in improving clinical outcomes.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Risk Factors and Etiology of Acute Mesenteric Ischemia (AMI)
items.content: AMIs are primarily due to embolic or thrombotic events, with approximately 50% caused by an embolus to the mid to distal superior mesenteric artery. Risk factors include atrial fibrillation, recent myocardial infarction, and other conditions leading to hypercoagulability. Nonocclusive ischemia accounts for 20% of AMI cases, often associated with acute hemodynamic instability.
keyPoints: Approximately 50% of AMI is due to an embolus to the mid to distal SMA.
Risk factors include atrial fibrillation and recent myocardial infarction.
Nonocclusive mesenteric ischemia represents 20% of cases, often linked with acute hemodynamic instability.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Understanding Collateral Circulation and Vulnerable Areas in Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency
items.content: The intestines receive blood supply from the celiac artery, SMA, and IMA. Extensive collateralization occurs between these arteries and their branches, meeting at Griffiths' point (duodenum/pancreas) and Sudeck's point (splenic flexure/descending/sigmoid colon). These areas are prone to decreased blood flow and colonic ischemia.
keyPoints: Collateral vessels meet at Griffiths' point and Sudeck's point, which are common sites for colonic ischemia.
The splanchnic circulation can receive up to 30% of cardiac output.
Protective responses include collateralization, autoregulation of blood flow, and increased oxygen extraction.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · ANATOMY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Distinguishing Occlusive from Non-Occlusive Ischemia in Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency
items.content: Occlusive ischemia results from emboli or thrombosis, often originating from the heart and lodging in the SMA. Chronic intestinal angina requires stenosis of two major vessels, with SMA involvement being most critical. Non-occlusive ischemia involves mesenteric vasoconstriction due to severe stress like shock, leading to mucosal ulceration and potential full-thickness injury if untreated.
keyPoints: >75% of emboli in occlusive ischemia originate from the heart, lodging distal to the middle colic artery origin.
Chronic intestinal angina requires stenosis of two major vessels supplying the intestine.
Non-occlusive ischemia is due to disproportionate mesenteric vasoconstriction and can lead to full-thickness injury if untreated.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · ANATOMY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Diagnosis and Initial Management of Chronic Mesenteric Ischemia
items.content: Patients with chronic mesenteric ischemia (CMI) present with recurrent postprandial epigastric pain, fear of eating leading to weight loss, and possibly chronic diarrhea. Symptoms typically last 6–12 months. Prompt diagnostic evaluation is crucial to exclude other causes like gastrointestinal malignancies.
keyPoints: Recurrent episodes of acute dull, crampy, postprandial epigastric pain
Fear of eating resulting in weight loss
Chronic diarrhea may be present
Symptoms typically last 6–12 months
High peak velocity on mesenteric duplex scan has ~80% positive predictive value for mesenteric ischemia
Negative duplex scan virtually precludes the diagnosis of mesenteric ischemia
CT angiography is the gold standard for assessing atherosclerotic disease
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.title: Role of Duplex Ultrasound and CT Angiography in CMI Diagnosis
items.content: Duplex ultrasound is valuable for screening mesenteric vessels due to its high sensitivity and specificity. A high peak velocity on the SMA indicates a strong likelihood of ischemia, while a negative result rules it out. CT angiography remains the definitive diagnostic tool.
keyPoints: Duplex ultrasound has high sensitivity and specificity
High peak velocity in SMA ~80% positive predictive value for mesenteric ischemia
Negative duplex scan virtually precludes diagnosis of mesenteric ischemia
CT angiography is the gold standard for assessing atherosclerotic disease
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.title: Management Strategies for Chronic Mesenteric Ischemia
items.content: Management of CMI involves aggressive medical therapy and individualized revascularization strategies. Endovascular approaches are preferred for suitable lesions, while open or hybrid techniques like ROMS are considered when endovascular methods fail.
keyPoints: Aggressive medical therapy includes cessation of smoking, antiplatelet, and lipid-lowering medications
Endovascular approach recommended first-line for short segment stenosis with minimal to moderate calcification
80% long-term success rate with angioplasty and endovascular stenting
Open revascularization considered for severe calcification or failed endovascular interventions
Retrograde open mesenteric stenting (ROMS) combines aspects of open surgery and percutaneous therapy
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Acute Mesenteric Ischemia: High Suspicion Despite Nonspecific Presentation
items.content: Acute mesenteric ischemia (AMI) often presents with severe abdominal pain that is disproportionately intense compared to physical findings. This discrepancy arises because ischemia begins in the mucosal layer and progresses outward, leading to significant pain without immediate external signs.
keyPoints: Severe, acute, nonremitting abdominal pain occurs in 95% of cases.
Pain is strikingly out of proportion to physical findings due to progression from mucosal to serosal layers.
Associated symptoms include nausea (44%), vomiting (35%), diarrhea (35%), and blood per rectum (16%).
High index of suspicion required for diagnosis despite nonspecific presentation.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic and Therapeutic Urgency in Acute Mesenteric Ischemia
items.content: AMIs are surgical emergencies necessitating immediate intervention. CT angiography with thin cuts is the gold standard for detecting mesenteric arterial occlusion. Surgical consultation should be sought promptly, and if embolic occlusion of the SMA is confirmed, surgery to restore blood flow should not be delayed.
keyPoints: CT angiography with 1-mm or thinner cuts is the diagnostic gold standard.
Immediate surgical exploration required upon confirmation of acute embolic occlusion of the SMA.
Heparin bolus and therapeutic drip initiated immediately in suspected AMI cases.
Operative goals include resecting nonviable bowel, restoring blood supply, and preserving viable bowel.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: What is the most common cause of acute mesenteric ischemia (AMI)?
items.explanation: Approximately 50% of AMI is due to an embolus to the mid to distal superior mesenteric artery.

items.subtopic: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: Which condition accounts for less than 10% of mesenteric ischemia cases?
items.explanation: Mesenteric venous thrombosis accounts for less than 10% of cases.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.question: What is the incidence rate of intestinal ischemia per 100,000 people?
items.explanation: Mesenteric ischemia affects 2–3 people per 100,000.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which artery is most commonly involved in embolic occlusive ischemia of the intestines?
items.explanation: Embolus preferentially lodges in the SMA just distal to the origin of the middle colic artery.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · ANATOMY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: Where do collateral vessels within the small bowel meet?
options: In the bed of the pancreas
items.explanation: Collateral vessels within the small bowel meet in the duodenum and the bed of the pancreas.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · ANATOMY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: What is known as Griffiths' point?
options: The site where collateral vessels within the colon meet
An area at risk for decreased blood flow in the small bowel
An area at risk for decreased blood flow in the duodenum and pancreas bed
An area at risk for decreased blood flow in the splenic flexure
items.explanation: Griffiths' point is an area inherently at risk for decreased blood flow, located where collateral vessels within the small bowel meet.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · ANATOMY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: Which diagnostic tool is considered the gold standard for assessing atherosclerotic disease of the aortic and visceral vessels in mesenteric ischemia?
items.explanation: Thin-sliced CT angiography is the gold standard for assessing atherosclerotic disease of the aortic and visceral vessels.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.question: What symptom classically presents in patients with Chronic Mesenteric Ischemia (CMI) as 'intestinal angina'?
items.explanation: 'Intestinal angina' refers to acute dull, crampy, postprandial epigastric pain.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.question: What is the approximate positive predictive value of mesenteric ischemia when a high peak velocity of flow in the SMA is demonstrated by a mesenteric duplex scan?
items.explanation: A high peak velocity of flow in the SMA on a mesenteric duplex scan has an ~80% positive predictive value for mesenteric ischemia.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.question: Which approach is recommended as first-line therapy for patients with suitable lesions requiring revascularization in CMI?
items.explanation: An endovascular approach is recommended as the first-line therapy for suitable lesions in CMI.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.question: What is the most common complaint in patients with AMI resulting from arterial embolus or thrombosis?
items.explanation: The most common complaint is severe, acute, nonremitting abdominal pain occurring in 95% of cases.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.question: Which diagnostic modality is considered the gold standard for detecting mesenteric arterial occlusive disease?
options: CT angiography with 1-mm or thinner cuts
items.explanation: CT angiography with 1-mm or thinner cuts is the gold standard for detecting mesenteric arterial occlusive disease.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.question: In patients with suspected AMI, what immediate pharmacological intervention should be administered?
items.explanation: Patients with suspected AMI should be given a heparin bolus immediately and started on a therapeutic heparin drip.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: The incidence of mesenteric venous thrombosis is higher than that of nonocclusive mesenteric ischemia.
items.explanation: Mesenteric venous thrombosis accounts for less than 10% of cases, while nonocclusive mesenteric ischemia represents 20%.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: Chronic mesenteric ischemia is caused by atherosclerotic disease leading to narrowing of the superior mesenteric artery.
items.explanation: CMI occurs due to significant atherosclerotic disease leading to the narrowing of the SMA and/or celiac artery origins.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency | INCIDENCE AND EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: The splanchnic circulation can receive up to 30% of cardiac output.
items.explanation: The passage states that the splanchnic circulation receives up to 30% of cardiac output.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · ANATOMY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.statement: Non-occlusive ischemia is caused by an embolus or thrombosis in a major artery.
items.explanation: Non-occlusive ischemia results from mesenteric vasoconstriction due to severe physiologic stress, not an embolus or thrombosis.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · ANATOMY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.statement: A negative duplex scan virtually precludes the diagnosis of mesenteric ischemia.
items.explanation: A negative duplex scan makes the diagnosis of mesenteric ischemia very unlikely.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.statement: The mortality rate for Acute Mesenteric Ischemia (AMI) is less than 50%.
items.explanation: The mortality rate of AMI is greater than 50%.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.statement: CT angiography findings are always necessary before surgical intervention in cases of suspected AMI.
items.explanation: Surgical therapy should not be delayed by additional diagnostic modalities despite their usefulness.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.statement: Patients with embolic ischemia are typically older adults with predisposing conditions like atrial fibrillation.
items.explanation: Embolic ischemia often occurs in older adults with conditions such as atrial fibrillation.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.assertion: Involvement of the SMA is most worrisome in occlusive ischemia.
items.reason: Embolus preferentially lodges in the SMA just distal to the origin of the middle colic artery.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why involvement of the SMA is most worrisome.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · ANATOMY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.assertion: Retrograde open mesenteric stenting (ROMS) combines aspects of both traditional open surgical bypass and percutaneous endovascular therapy.
items.reason: It involves a laparotomy for direct bowel assessment and SMA exposure using a retrograde approach.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why ROMS combines aspects of both approaches.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.assertion: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy is used as a screening tool for evaluating mesenteric vessels.
items.reason: Duplex ultrasound has gained popularity due to its high sensitivity and specificity.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; esophagogastroduodenoscopy is not used for evaluating mesenteric vessels.
items.reference: Mesenteric Vascular Insufficiency · PRESENTATION, EVALUATION, AND MANAGEMENT (part 1)
